perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees and undertakes that it shall not, without first obtaining the written consent of the other, disclose or make available to any person, reproduce or transmit in arty manner, or use (directly or indirectly) for its own benefit or the benefit of others, any Confidential Information save and except both parties may disclose any Confidential Information to their Affiliates, directors, officers, employees or advisors of their own or of Affiliates on a “need to know” basis to enable them to evaluate such Confidential Information in connection with the negotiation of the possible business relationship; provided that such persons have been informed of, and agree to be bound by obligations which are at least as strict as the recipient’s obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees and undertakes to use reasonable efforts to ensure that it does not disclose or make available to any person, reproduce or transmit in any manner, or use (directly or indirectly) for its own benefit or the benefit of others, any Confidential Information; both parties may disclose any Confidential Information to their Affiliates, directors, officers, employees or advisors of their own or of Affiliates on a “need to know” basis.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'use reasonable efforts to ensure that it does not' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.  Removing the requirement that those receiving confidential information be bound by obligations as strict as the recipient introduces further ambiguity and weakens protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce.  The modified text, by allowing for less stringent protection of confidential information, could enable the use of that information in a way that unfairly harms the disclosing party's business, potentially violating this Massachusetts law. The 'reasonable efforts' standard introduces too much subjectivity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text uses the phrase 'shall not,' imposing a strict obligation. The changed text uses 'use reasonable efforts to ensure that it does not,' weakening this obligation and introducing ambiguity. Further, it removes the requirement that those receiving the information be bound by obligations at least as strict as the recipient's, further reducing protection of confidential information. According to the Law Explanation, this could violate M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, which prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. The scraped law snippets (Snippet 1 and 2) both state that 'Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two...' may bring an action. By weakening confidentiality protections, the modified text increases the risk of unfair competition through misuse of confidential information, potentially leading to loss of money or property.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a like nature, and in no event such degree of care and protection shall be of less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall endeavor to use a degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it deems appropriate, but in no event will it be grossly negligent.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall use the same degree of care and protection' with 'shall endeavor to use a degree of care and protection as it deems appropriate' makes the protection of confidential information subjective.  Allowing a 'grossly negligent' standard is insufficient for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 covers the misappropriation of trade secrets, which are defined as confidential information that gives a business a competitive edge. By using weaker language, the receiving party would not be required to adequately protect trade secrets, leading to potential misappropriation. The law requires a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy which 'endeavor to use a degree of care and protection as it deems appropriate' does not guarantee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text mandates using 'the same degree of care and protection' but the modified text only requires the receiving party to 'endeavor to use a degree of care and protection as it deems appropriate,' which introduces subjectivity. Further, allowing a 'grossly negligent' standard is insufficient, contradicting the reasonable care implied in trade secret protection, as described in the Law Explanation, and potentially leading to misappropriation as defined in M.G.L. c. 93 § 42, which discusses improper means, including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information.' By lowering the standard of care, the modified text increases the risk of such a breach.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to indemnify and keep indemnified each other against all loss and damage, which the disclosing party may suffer as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; provided always that the disclosing party shall forthwith give written notice to the receiving party of the above loss and damage and satisfactory documentary evidence of such actual loss and damage.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to potentially indemnify each other for provable direct loss and damage. The disclosing party should provide notice and documentation of loss, if feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to indemnify and keep indemnified' to 'agree to potentially indemnify' makes indemnification uncertain. Replacing 'shall forthwith give written notice' with 'should provide notice...if feasible' weakens the requirement for prompt notification of losses.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 prohibits unfair or deceptive business practices. A failure to provide clear indemnification or a prompt notification of losses resulting from a breach of the agreement may constitute an unfair or deceptive practice, particularly if the disclosing party suffers significant harm as a result of the receiving party's actions and the receiving party refuses to provide any recourse.

Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding indemnification and weakens the requirement for prompt notification of losses. Specifically, changing 'agree to indemnify and keep indemnified' to 'agree to potentially indemnify' makes the indemnification obligation uncertain. Similarly, replacing 'shall forthwith give written notice' with 'should provide notice...if feasible' diminishes the mandatory nature of notifying the receiving party about losses. The LLM Law Explanation suggests this could be seen as an unfair business practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11. While the snippets do not explicitly define 'unfair or deceptive act or practice,' they establish that actions resulting in loss of money or property due to such practices are actionable. By weakening the indemnification and notice requirements, the modified text increases the potential for the disclosing party to suffer losses without recourse, potentially violating the standard established by M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information coming to the knowledge of the other may relate to and/or have implications regarding the future strategies, plans, business activities, methods, processes and or information of the parties, which afford them certain competitive and strategic advantage. Accordingly neither party shall use the Confidential Information in a manner that will jeopardise or adversely affect in any manner such future strategies, plans, business activities, methods, processes, information, and/or competitive and strategic advantage of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information may relate to future strategies. Accordingly, neither party should intentionally use the Confidential Information to cause significant harm to the disclosing party's business.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'neither party shall use...in a manner that will jeopardise or adversely affect' to 'neither party should intentionally use...to cause significant harm' introduces a higher, more subjective standard for breach. The term 'significant harm' is vague and open to interpretation, making it difficult to prove a violation. The original text was broader, preventing any action that would adversely affect a business, instead of just those that cause significant harm, and by changing the term to 'should' instead of 'shall' makes the requirement advisory.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. This change makes it much more difficult to establish that trade secrets have been misappropriated, as it requires proving intentionality and 'significant harm', which are subjective and hard to demonstrate. The original text provided broader protection against any use of the confidential information that could negatively impact the disclosing party's competitive advantage.

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The change from 'neither party shall use...in a manner that will jeopardise or adversely affect' to 'neither party should intentionally use...to cause significant harm' weakens the protection of confidential information, creating an ambiguity that undermines legal enforceability under M.G.L. c. 93 § 42. The original text created a clear obligation, whereas the modified text introduces the vague standard of 'significant harm' and the lower standard of 'should' instead of 'shall'. The snippets define 'misappropriation' under Section 42 as including 'disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent'. By requiring proof of 'intentional' use and 'significant harm,' the changed text sets a higher bar than the law requires to prove misappropriation, thus contradicting the statute's protection against unauthorized use of trade secrets.
